skill.name: ai-runtime-memory
skill.description: Layered AI runtime memory system for recording project context, querying markdown event history, and preserving decisions across sessions.
skill.summary: Query and maintain markdown-based AI runtime memory across short-term, long-term, and episodic records.
skill.author: Dwsy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, weak-crypto, obfuscation, and network indicators. Review found most hits are markdown examples, Chinese text entropy, path references, or false weak-crypto matches, but the skill includes an executable shell wrapper and documented file deletion and backup commands. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found, so publication is reasonable with clear warnings about local command and filesystem access.

medium_findings.title: Executable Local Shell Wrapper
medium_findings.description: The skill includes a Bash helper that invokes python3 and local shell tools. This appears intended for local memory querying, but users should treat it as executable code and review commands before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The executable script directly calls local commands and accepts user arguments. The purpose is legitimate, but the behavior is confirmed and requires user trust.

medium_findings.title: Documented Destructive Filesystem Maintenance
medium_findings.description: The maintenance guide recommends commands that delete short-term memory files and create backups. These are normal administration tasks, but copied commands can remove user data if run in the wrong directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file deletion command is present in documentation and targets the memory directory. It is not hidden or malicious, but it is a real data-loss risk.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Project and User Context Storage
medium_findings.description: The memory files are designed to store project state, recent files, user relationship notes, and preferences. This is core functionality, but it can retain sensitive context in markdown files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic purpose of the skill is persistent memory, and the cited files show stored context and user preference data. This is legitimate but privacy-sensitive.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Markdown and Text
low_findings.description: Many reported weak cryptography, high entropy, and Ruby backtick findings are caused by markdown file extensions, Chinese prose, command examples, and formatted documentation rather than active cryptographic or obfuscated code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited files shows natural-language documentation and code fences, not encrypted payloads or weak cryptographic implementations.

low_findings.title: Non-Executable Network Badge
low_findings.description: The only confirmed hardcoded external URL reviewed is a README badge image. It does not create runtime network behavior in the skill tools.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is embedded in markdown as a display badge. No active network client code was found in the reviewed Python or shell tools.

low_findings.title: Relative Path References Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The reported path traversal sequences in README are relative links to nearby project documentation. They are not used by executable code to access arbitrary files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is plain markdown links. No file-open or shell operation uses these paths in the inspected code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Execution in Helper Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper script runs local commands including python3, grep, find, and wc. This is expected for a CLI helper, but it means the skill should be reviewed before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are directly present in an executable script. They are local and purposeful, so this is a controlled but real pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Scanning of Markdown Memory Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The Python discovery module recursively scans markdown files under the episodic memory directory and reads their contents. This is core functionality but can expose local memory data in output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The recursive scan and file reads are explicit in the implementation. The root path is scoped to the memory directory, reducing severity.

content.value_statement: AI assistants lose project decisions and context between sessions. This skill provides markdown memory layers and query tools for persistent project history.
actual_capabilities: Documents a three-layer memory model for short-term, long-term, and episodic project records.
Queries episodic markdown files with SQL-style filters for dates, tags, types, and timestamps.
Formats query results as readable tables or JSON for downstream analysis.
Provides a Bash helper for common searches, recent activity, type counts, tag counts, and statistics.
Includes maintenance guidance for cleaning, backing up, and reviewing memory records.
limitations: The query language supports only a small subset of SQL-style expressions.
The shell helper assumes a POSIX-like environment with Python 3 available.
Memory contents are stored as local markdown and can include sensitive context.
Some documentation references larger AI Runtime folders that are not included in this package.

use_cases.title: Review Recent Project Decisions
use_cases.description: Find recent architecture, security, or planning decisions across markdown memory records.

use_cases.title: Maintain Assistant Context
use_cases.description: Record working state, preferences, and project structure so future AI sessions can regain context faster.

use_cases.title: Audit Memory Quality
use_cases.description: Inspect memory files for missing titles, timestamps, tags, or inconsistent event records.

prompt_templates.title: Show Recent Memory
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-runtime-memory to summarize the most recent project events and list any decisions that need follow-up.

prompt_templates.title: Find Tagged Decisions
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the episodic memory for decisions tagged architecture or security, then summarize the date, title, and impact of each result.

prompt_templates.title: Check Memory Health
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the memory structure and identify stale short-term notes, missing metadata, and records that should move into long-term memory.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Project Retrospective
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the short-term, long-term, and episodic memory files to create a retrospective of major decisions, unresolved risks, and reusable lessons.

output_examples.input: Find architecture decisions from the last week.
output: A dated list of matching decision records.
A short summary of each decision and its recorded impact.
Follow-up items when the memory notes mention open work.

output_examples.input: Review memory quality for this project.
output: A concise health report covering timestamps, titles, tags, and duplicate records.
Suggested cleanup actions for short-term and long-term memory files.

output_examples.input: Summarize what the assistant should remember before the next session.
output: A session handoff with current task state, important context, user preferences, and next actions.

best_practices: Review memory files before sharing a repository because they can contain private project context.
Use tags and timestamps consistently so queries return useful results.
Run deletion and backup commands from the expected project root only.
anti_patterns: Do not store secrets, tokens, or private credentials in memory markdown files.
Do not treat the SQL-style filter as a complete SQL implementation.
Do not run maintenance commands without checking the target directory first.

faq.question: What does this skill store?
faq.answer: It stores project memory as markdown files for current context, long-term knowledge, and historical events.

faq.question: Does it need a database?
faq.answer: No. The included tools scan markdown files and parse YAML front matter locally.

faq.question: Can it query all SQL syntax?
faq.answer: No. It supports a limited SQL-style filter syntax for common memory lookups.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish memory files?
faq.answer: Review them first. Memory files can contain user preferences, project details, and internal decisions.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The package includes an executable shell helper and documentation with filesystem maintenance commands.
